items.title: Trino on Kubernetes: Speed,Flexibility,and Control Beyond AWS Athena -TadehAlexani | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>When serverless analytics starts to slow you down and costs more than it should, it’s time to take control. We transitioned from AWS Athena to a self-managed, open-source Trino cluster on Kubernetes for enhanced speed, flexibility, and cost efficiency.

This wasn’t just a migration; it was a full re‑architecture built for performance. Running Trino on Kubernetes, entirely on Spot instances, let us tune, scale, and optimize for real workloads. The payoff? Faster queries, up to 50% lower costs, and the freedom to shape our data platform without vendor constraints.

We’ll share how we made open‑source Trino production‑ready, from query tuning and autoscaling to keeping it stable in a dynamic cloud environment. If you’re hitting the limits of managed services or want to reclaim speed and control, this talk shows what it takes to run Trino at scale: fast, flexible, and fully in your hands.</article>

items.title: Working as a Staff Engineer on one of Germany’s largest software projects -M. Bohn | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content:
<article>Replacing a decades-old legacy system during operations is like changing a Formula 1 car's engine during a race—you can’t stop and nothing can go wrong. 

These systems are business-critical and too large and entangled to replace with a big bang. 

We believe this is the pinnacle of software engineering: reverse engineering an old system, often written in an ancient language, and reengineering it with new technologies without hiccups or outages. 

The goal is to remove what is no longer needed, add missing features, and keep the core intact. How do you organize hundreds of engineers to create a uniform, maintainable code base that can last for decades? Because the system is so large, it will never be fully replaced again. How can we deliver exactly the needed functionality with quality so high that new versions can be rolled out daily to tens of thousands of global users? 

Michael will share insights into the role of a Staff Engineer in system design, development, and execution.</article>

items.title: Context is (almost) all you need - Kevin Heinicke | code.talks 2025
items.author: code.talks (ehem. Developer Conference)
items.content: <article>Encoding business intent directly in code. Fragmented context is slowing data & ML initiatives. The fix is to encode it directly in code, not in more docs or meetings. In this talk I'll showcase Deichmann's approach for predictive pricing and how we define context using Python's versatility and Pydantic's strictness. This creates focus and facilitates technical discussions – even in a business environment. It also accelerates onboarding, improves team communication, and allows for more effective AI prompting. At Deichmann, this allows us to develop and scale our pricing software to provide real value to the business and to our customers.</article>
